text: Of David, is not in Septuagint. But he probably composed this psalm to exculpate himself from the accusation of pride. (Berthier)<hr>It may agree with Esther, Nehemias, etc., 2 Esdras 5:15. (Calmet)<hr>David proposes his own humility to the imitation of others, without any evil intention. (Worthington)<hr>Sometimes the saints may speak their own praises, as St. Paul, did, particularly when they are inspired. (Berthier)<hr>Above me. This deportment is admirable in the great. (Calmet)<hr>We must neither undertake nor pry into things above our ability. (Menochius)

So reward, etc. The meaning is, that according to his disposition with regard to humility, so he expected a reward in his soul to return to him, and stick as close to him as the child just weaned, which would willingly never be separated from the mother. (Challoner)<hr>If I was not humble, may my soul be treated like an infant, etc. Hebrew, "If I did not render (or humble and silence) my soul, like an infant weaned from his mother, let my soul be to me as a weaned child." I willingly submit to all thy rigours, if I did not adore thy ways in silence and in humiliation. (Calmet)<hr>The text may be differently rendered. (Berthier)<hr>As children come willingly to their mother, so the child of God approaches to Him, though he may not always find the same sweetness; and he expects that he will be rewarded accordingly. (Worthington)<hr>Gemul means, "a weaned infant, or a reward." (Du Hamel)
Israel. This occurs in the preceding psalm. (Calmet)<hr>David encourages all to hope by his own experience. (Worthington)
